title: Evident: a Development Methodology and a Knowledge Base Topology for Data Mining, Machine Learning and General Knowledge Management [PDF]

paragraphs.text: ## **Evident: a Development Methodology and a Knowledge Base Topology for Data Mining, Machine Learning and General Knowledge Management**

## **Anonymous Author(s)**

Affiliation Address email

## **Abstract**

1 Software has been developed for knowledge discovery, prediction and management 2 for over 30 years. However, there are still unresolved pain points when using 3 existing project development and artifact management methodologies. Historically, 4 there has been a lack of applicable methodologies. Further, methodologies that 5 have been applied, such as Agile, have several limitations including scientific 6 unfalsifiability that reduce their applicability. _Evident_ , a development methodology 7 rooted in the philosophy of logical reasoning and _EKB_ , a knowledge base topology, 8 are proposed. Many pain points in data mining, machine learning and general 9 knowledge management are alleviated conceptually. _Evident_ can be extended 10 potentially to accelerate philosophical exploration, science discovery, education as 11 well as knowledge sharing & retention across the globe. _EKB_ offers one solution 12 of storing information as knowledge, a granular level above data. Related topics in 13 computer history, software engineering, database, sensing hardware, philosophy, 14 and project & organization & military managements are also discussed.

## 15 **1 Introduction**

- 16 Necessity is the mother of invention, claimed Plato [1]. Deficient in rigorous scientific scrutinization 17 as the statement is, major methodology evolutions in software development did not emerge until the 18 emergence of major computer innovations and thereafter elevated effort orchestration needs.

- 19 In the 1940s, digital programmable electronic computers revolutionized scientific calculation done 20 previously with mechanical and analog computing machines [2]. Assembly (1947) [3, 4] and high 21 level (1953) [5, 6] programming languages rose to harness the unprecedented and ever-increasing 22 computing power, Eventually the term software was coined (1953) [7]. Two Software Development 23 Methodologies (SDMs) were proposed: 1. a project breakdown of sequential phases, the essence of 24 Waterfall (see Fig 1a), first presented no later than 1956 [8] and 2. iterative and incremental SDM, 25 the essence of Agile (see Fib 1b), first executed no later than 1957 [9].

26 In the 1960s, operating systems (1962) emerged to orchestrate multiple computation tasks[10]. This 27 signified the shift of computer development from single-task specialized machines for military and 28 academia to machines accessible to the general public. The shift was exemplified by The Mother of 29 All Demos (1968) which demonstrated many fundamental elements of personal computing for the 30 first time [11] and showed how software had evolved in both diversity and complexity. Meanwhile, 31 the first formal detailed diagram of the Waterfall methodology appeared in literature (See Fig 1a) 32 (1970) [12] and the name of Waterfall was ultimately coined (1976) [13]. Agile variants such as 33 evolutionary project management [14] and adaptive SDM [15] appeared in the early 1970s, although 34 no clear preference between Waterfall and Agile variants was found in the literature.

- 37 In the 1980s, personal computers entered households[16] followed by graphic user interface (GUI) 38 (1983) [16]. The Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP) was standardized (1982)[17] and commercial 39 Internet service providers emerged (1989) [18, 19] Unprecedented user-computer interactions and 40 user-user communications created tremendous software needs, while Waterfall was still widely 41 deployed in software development. United States Department of Defense issued a military standard 42 describing Waterfall as the required military software development process (1985) [20]. However, 43 software user needs grew so fast that, the heavy Waterfall SDM failed to deliver in pace. Consequently, 44 a number of light weight SDMs were proposed and practiced (1990s) [4, 21, 22, 23]. Eventually The 45 Manifesto for Agile Software Development (The Manifesto) [24] was signed by 17 practitioners of 46 light-weight SDM (2001) and became the de facto SDM.

- 47 Around 1990, data storage capacities grew significantly and software usages in Data Mining (DM, 48 defined as knowledge discovery from data) reached the tipping point. While data and software’s 49 storage manners differ between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures, data storage capacity growth 50 empowered software to discover knowledge supported by scientific evidence (defined as Knowledge) 51 that people never had access to. Corporations started to analyze customers’ behavior and make 52 business decisions based on Knowledge (1990s) [25]. The first DM methodology, Cross-Industry 53 Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM) was conceived (1996) [26, 27]. However, CRISP54 DM and its variants appear more of a theoretical framework, offer little meaningful or actionable 55 guidance, and therefore have not gotten much traction. In addition, CRISP-DM is concerned only 56 with DM, not Machine Learning (ML, defined as to deliver an algorithm (Algo) for Knowledge 57 prediction) or Knowledge Management (KM) in general for science, medicine, military and so on. 58 Due to the absence of alternative methodologies(see Table 1), Agile is still being offered up for DM, 59 ML, and KM [28, 29] with questions being asked about its appropriateness [30, 31].

- 60 This paper discusses limitations in Agile as a scientific claim and why it may not address the current 61 pain points of DM, ML and KM, which are later summarized. _Evident_ along with _Evident Knowledge_ 62 _Base_ ( _EKB_ ) is proposed as a project development and artifact management methodology. _Evident_ ’s 63 potential in alleviating many current pain points is demonstrated conceptually. Unalleviated pain

2

- 64 points and future work to fulfill the potential are also discussed. Beyond software development, 65 _Evident_ is illustrated to be applicable in many aspects of society. _EKB_ is demonstrated as one potential 66 infrastructure to store information as Knowledge, a granular level above data.

## 67 **2 Agile ambiguity and unfasifiability**

- 68 Most people regard Agile as iterative, evolutionary and incremental software development [9] (see 69 Fig 1b) and many claim to be Agile practitioners. However, Agile empirical evidence is mixed and 70 hard to find [32, 30] while no measurable scientific evidence has been found at all. Although control 71 experiment challenges or absence of quantitative project Agility measurements may explain no 72 measurable scientific evidence, concerns remain with the ambiguity with which Agile’s approaches 73 and scope are defined in The Manifesto.

## 74 **2.1 Agile approaches are vaguely defined in The Manifesto**

- 75 The Manifesto includes 4 values and 12 principles [24]. The goal is crystal clear: to rapidly deliver 76 quality software that meets user needs, but not so much can be found for how to get there. Most of 77 the Values and Principles appear to be goals but not approaches (see Appendix); some are concerned 78 with approaches but vaguely defined; only four principles are actionable, which turn out to have no 79 relevance in how to implement iterative, evolutionary or incremental development. Agile Alliance, 80 co-founded by some original signers of The Manifesto, defines Agile as “an umbrella term for a set 81 of frameworks and practices” from which Agile practitioners “figure out the right things to do given 82 your particular context.” [33] Unfortunately, no actionable approaches are defined.

- 83 Therefore although there are numerous frameworks under the Agile umbrella [34, 35], it’s impossible 84 to determine if a development practice is Agile and the claim of Agile practice becomes unfalsifiable. 85 Because falsifiability is the standard evaluating scientific against non-scientific claims introduced by 86 Karl Popper [36], Agile is not a scientific claim. Consequently, no observable scientific evidence can 87 prove or disprove Agile, because technically no one can determine if a project is Agile or not in the 88 first place. If an Agile rollout “fails”, Agile proponents can always argue that the Agile rollout was 89 not implemented correctly.

- 90 Meanwhile Agile practitioners cannot determine if they practice Agile correctly either. Projects 91 employing Agile frameworks such as Test Driven Development or Feature Driven Development 92 may not even realize that the projects may not be adaptive to new user needs. People who are 93 essentially practicing Waterfall may believe they are practicing Agile only because they implement 94 each sequential Waterfall phase incrementally or simply use Scrum or Kanban.

- 95 In defense, some proponents claim Agile as a philosophy [37, 38]. Granted Agile’s goal may fit into 96 Axiology, one of Philosophy’s four domains (the rest as Metaphysics, Epistemology and Logic) [39], 97 concerned with what is good, it appears to be a common understanding and offers little value when 98 Agile’s approaches are vaguely defined.

## 99 **2.2 No scopes are defined in The Manifesto**

- 100 “The right things to do given your particular context” by Agile Alliance [33] are expected to be found 101 within Agile’s frameworks, otherwise Agile is not practiced right. With no scope defined, Agile 102 seems to cover the scope of all softwares. However, some softwares have non-incremental needs or 103 simply only one need, e.g. to solve one specific partial differential equation numerically. Their needs 104 are either met or not at all. No iteration or evolutionary Agile design cycles exist.

- 105 Agile is also not applicable for Knowledge discovery tasks such as DM. In a typical Agile development 106 cycle (Fig 1b), Review phase is to discover Knowledge about user needs, which can be done through 107 DM. Therefore Agile should not be applicable to DM, one phase of its own design cycle. Table 2: Pain Points of DM, ML and KM.

## 108 **3 Pain points for DM, ML and KM**

109 Owing to the absence of applicable methodologies, pain points have been continuously reported for 110 DM, ML and KM [40, 41, 42, 43] (see Table 2) in the current big data era with explosive growth in 111 data volume, variety and velocity.

112 DM & ML’s Algo typically comprises a data computation flow (defined as a Model, supervised 113 or unsupervised), such as logistic regression, and its configuration, such as logistic regression 114 coefficients. DM employs off-the-shelf or in-house Models to discover Knowledge from data. ML 115 compares Knowledges discovered by DM by candidate models and deploys the one that performs best 116 with its configuration, as the production Algo, for Knowledge prediction in production. Therefore, 117 DM’s pain points still apply to ML. Meanwhile because DM and ML are special forms of KM, their 118 pain points are also applicable for KM (see Fig 2).

119 Generally speaking, DM has not been regarded highly collaborative and scalable activities to deliver 120 high throughput Knowledge. It’s rare to see hundreds of contributors in a DM project, unlike for 121 example some complicated open source software projects that, deliver promptly, efficiently and 122 continuously for years or even decades [44, 45]. It is also rare of mass Knowledge production 123 in a organized and standardized manner with high production yield for a unit period, commonly 124 seen in consumer products such as automobiles or toothpastes. DM often struggles to deliver 125 Knowledge rapidly with technical debts in reproducibility, measurability, trackability. DM needs 126 to not only handle artifacts of different modalities such as documents, codes and data, but also 127 address computation and data storage resource requests potentially across multiple platforms. Raw or 128 preprocessed data can be compromised in availability, accuracy and consistency. Data dependency 129 and entangled models often cause cascaded correction and uncertainty in project planning. In addition, 130 routine tasks such as quarterly or annual finance analysis mostly have not be automated. Tools and 131 project management methodologies are highly in demand to fulfill DM’s potential and deliver values.

132 In ML, the team members, usually software engineers or product managers, that deploy an Algo in 133 production to predict future data may not have produced the Algo and may misuse it. The Algo codes 134 are often refactored sometimes in different programming languages, operating systems or even in 135 fixed point instead of float point. If the Algo is to be deployed on a data acquisition product such 136 as cameras or bio-sensors, no product grade data are available for Algo research until the sensor 137 hardware design is finalized to enable data collection. Algo research therefore becomes the bottleneck 138 for product release. Frequently sub-optimal Algo is deployed to meet the deadline or projects become 139 delayed. What’s more, production data may come from different sources compared to research data 140 potentially caused by, e.g. sensor upgrade or downgrade, resulting in the under performance of 141 production Algo. After Algo deployment, no straightforward way exists to monitor Algo performance 142 or thereafter update Algo. Future Knowledge predicated by the deployed Algo may encourage 143 Algo users to alter decisions, which leads to the formation of future data with unanticipated hidden 144 feedback loops. In addition, prototype Algo or dead codes may be accidentally run in production 145 potentially causing catastrophic consequences.

146 Although scientific methods have guided people to discover Knowledge and improve practices such 147 as evidence-based medicine [46] and experiment based military development [47], people still form 148 Knowledge that lacks in supporting evidence [48]. One possible reason is the unavailability of tools 149 or resources to check if the Knowledge formed is well supported by evidence, especially when there 150 is a significant time gap between the Knowledge formed and the appearance of supporting evidence, 151 e.g. for long-term investments or corporation strategies. Knowledge dissemination and retention 152 are also huge challenges among the community and organization [49]. Furthermore, Knowledge is 153 mostly recorded in the form of articles. However, because articles writing has not been and probably 154 will never be standardized, Knowledge has not been able to be represented in a standardized manner 155 for definition, reference and storage. The same Knowledge recorded in different sentences or even 156 languages may be interpreted differently.

5

## 157 **4** _**Evident**_ **: a project development and artifact management methodology**

## 158 **4.1 Definition and scope**
159 _Evident_ is a methodology of project, including but not limited to software, development and artifact 160 management for DM, ML and KM, characterized by

- 161 a. project development mimicking a continuous process of logical reasoning in philosophy;

- 162 b. project activities or artifacts are broken into containers of Observations, Hypotheses and 163 Tests (collectively defined as Containers);
- 164 c. directional association constructions towards and only towards Test Containers to represent 165 Knowledge.

166 Observation is a collection of facts. A Hypothesis is Knowledge to be formed out of Observation. A 167 Test is a Hypothesis evaluation process using Observation to prove or disprove the Hypothesis with 168 or without confidence levels. Containers indicate Observations, Hypotheses and Tests can only be 169 added or removed as a block.

170

## 171 **4.2 Knowledge representation**

172 Loosely speaking, a Test associated with a Hypothesis and an Observation represents induction 173 Knowledge (see Fig 3a); a Test associated with a Hypothesis set and an Observation represents 174 abduction Knowledge (see Fig 3b), a Hypothesis associated Test that is also associated with an 175 induction or abduction Knowledge Test represents deduction Knowledge or prediction (see Fig 3a&b). 176 Deduction Knowledge becomes induction Knowledge once Observation proving or disproving 177 deduction Knowledge is associated with Test, while stay deducted Knowledge if the associated 178 Observation overlooks (fails to either prove or disprove), the deduction Knowledge. Multiple 179 Tests associated with the same pair of Hypothesis(es) and Observation represent multiple different 180 Knowledges based on different evaluation metrics (e.g. profit maximization or cost minimization) or 181 Observation usage strategies (e.g. cross-validation grouping) (see Fig 3c).

- 182 DM may be regarded as Knowledge induction with data as Observation, model as Hypothesis to 183 be evaluated and data analysis experiment as model test on data to form induction Knowledge with 184 statistical confidence (see Fig 4a). Similarly, ML is Knowledge abduction. An example is a data 185 analysis experiment that picks the best off-the-shelf or in-house model that best explains the data 186 to form the Algo for production (see Fig 4b). Experiments employing different cost functions (e.g.

6

187 RMSE, AUC or correlation coefficients), statistical confidence levels or data allocation strategies for 188 training and testing may result in different Knowledges or Algos.

## 189 **4.3 Project development**

190 _Evident_ project developments are intuitively broken down into two granular levels: Knowledges 191 and Containers. Mimicking logical reasoning in philosophy, each project period develops a batch 192 of independent Knowledges or Containers assigned to teams of various sizes to maximize unit time 193 throughput (see Fig 5). The next batches of Knowledge or Containers can be adaptively planned 194 after period reviews or retrieved from backlogs. _Evident_ is compatible with Kanban, Scrum or other 195 development tools or frameworks for project planning and development of a single Knowledge or 196 Container.Any tools or frameworks that do not compromise the project breakdown into Knowledge 197 and Containers are applicable.

198

## 199 **4.4 Artifact management:** _**EKB**_

200 _Evident_ artifact management may build on a topology of a relational Knowledge base, named 201 _EKB_ , composed of _Evident_ Containers and directional associations (see Fig 6). Knowledges can 202 be reproduced by Containers stored in _EKB_ . Oversimplified as a table, _EKB_ columns represent 203 Observations; rows represent Hypotheses; values represent Tests or Test to be done (TBD). A Test 204 can only be associated with one Observation, even if the associated Observation overlooks the 205 Hypothesis(es) associated with the Test. Any new Observation proving, disproving or overlooking 206 the same Hypothesis(es) occupies a column in _EKB_ .

## 207 **4.4.1** _**EKB**_ **stores containers and Knowledges continuously**

208 When new Hypotheses, Observations or deduction Knowledge Tests are developed, new rows or 209 columns of TBDs are inserted. A Test associated with Hypothesis(es) and a Observation can be 210 stored in the designated row and column to represent different Knowledges.

211 An induction Knowledge Test is placed in the row of its associated Hypothesis and the column of its 212 associated Observation (see Fig 3a&c & Fig 6). An abduction Knowledge Test is placed in the row 213 of the Hypothesis best explaining the Observation (see Fig 3b & Fig 6). A deduction Knowledge 214 is placed in the row of its associated Hypothesis and the column of the pending Observation (see 215 Fig 3a&b & Fig 6). Once an Observation becomes available proving or disproving the Hypothesis, 216 a deduction Knowledge becomes an induction Knowledge. Multiple Tests representing different 217 Knowledges can be placed in the same slot (see Fig 3c & Fig 6).

## 218 **4.4.2** _**EKB**_ **supports relational database operations of Permutation And Join**

219 _EKB_ is similar to a relational database [50] with Observations as columns, Hypotheses as rows and 220 Tests as values, but with potential associations among values for deduction Knowledges. Relational 221 database operations independent of values associations, such as Permutation (switching rows and 222 columns) and Join (merging _EKB_ s) can be implemented without compromise in _EKB_ ; operations 223 dependent on values associations such as Restriction (select rows), Projection (select columns) and 224 Compositions (merge selected columns&rows from multiple _EKB_ s) can only be implemented for

- 225 _EKB_ s storing only induction or abduction Knowledge and have no associations among Tests.

7

- 226 _EKB_ s are highly flexible for team collaboration and maintenance. Different team members working 227 on different Containers can share one _EKB_ as the common work space to improve efficiency. Multiple 228 _EKB_ s can be joined together without information loss so that Knowledges produced by different 229 teams or team members can be accumulated into one _EKB_ . For _EKB_ s storing only induction and 230 abduction Knowledge, all relational database operations are applicable, so that team members can 231 compose their own _EKB_ s without keeping a potentially large team _EKB_ on the local machines.

## 232 **5 Advantages and pain points alleviated**

- 233 Inspired by the philosophy of logical reasoning, _Evident_ is intuitive to understand and follow. Project 234 activity and artifact containerization supports incremental as well as adaptive project planning and 235 artifact pattern abstraction. Disentangling Hypotheses and Observations reduces unnecessary de236 pendency, cascade correction and uncertainty in project planning. Knowledge representation in 237 associations among artifacts can not only track Knowledge development, but also Knowledge devel238 opment status (prove, disproved or overlooked), which improves project measurability, trackability 239 and reproducibility. Overall _Evident_ may help applicable projects deliver fast and at scale with many 240 pain points alleviated in DM, ML and KM (see Table 2).

## 241 **5.1 DM**

- 242 Containerized Data and Models in _Evident_ prevents unstable data dependency, model entanglement 243 and cascade correction. Dead data and codes can be easily identified and removed. Standardized 244 Models and Experiments encourage reuse of computationally efficient containers, support automatic 245 DM. Different experiments may use different optimization target function on the same Model and 246 Data to deliver different Knowledges for different users, e.g. Marketing vs Engineering managers.

- 247 Containerization is an alternative to the state of the art artifact version control, such as git, which 248 keeps only the one version of the code or data in the workspace with historic versions saved as 249 commits. _Evident_ keeps all applicable versions available in the workspace for easy access. This may 250 appear to use more storage space. However current version control tools all save version commits as 251 snapshots [51], demanding comparable storage space of _Evident_ if a _Evident_ equivalent number of 252 versions are stored.

- 253 _Evident_ granulates project activities into independent standardized Knowledge and Container levels, 254 supports adaptive development, facilitates project planning among collaborators in teams of various 255 sizes and reduces planning overhead. Artifacts are continuously stored in _EKB_ , making project 256 development measurable, trackable, reproducible and scalable. Meanwhile once Containers are 257 produced, Knowledge or documentation reports can be generated automatically instead of manually. 258 _Evident_ accelerates DM delivery in both short-term and long-term.

## 259 **5.2 ML**

- 260 A deployed Algo can be evaluated easily by re-applying the original Research Experiment on the 261 production data. Different users involved in the deployment can understand the Algo’s scope and 262 origins easily by examining the research Experiment (see Fig 4b). Research Experiments can load 263 data in real time as Production Experiment, so that both Experiments can inherit the same design 264 patterns with statistical analysis and evaluation metrics. The Production Experiment can report and 265 examine the prediction performance at regular time intervals to detect production data pattern drift 266 for either model reconfiguration or model replacement. Once a model with its configuration is retired 267 from production, the production data is containerized and associated with the Production Experiment, 268 transforming the Production Experiment into Research Experiment and a deduction Knowledge for 269 prediction into an induction Knowledge that is also preserved in _EKB_ .

- 270 Because both research and production can operate on the same _EKB_ , research and production team 271 members can share the same workspace the way software engineers work on the same code repository, 272 facilitating the model migration from research to production and efficient team collaborations.

8

## 273 **5.3 KM**

274 Knowledge formatting into design patterns of Containers provides a meaningful progress towards 275 Knowledge standardization for improved definition, reference and storage compared to state of art 276 sentences or articles. _EKB_ with standardized Container templates may offer potential tools for people 277 to examine the Hypotheses formed against evidence or Observations, facilitating evidence-based 278 decision making and Knowledge development. _EKB_ can not only facilitate Knowledge dissemination, 279 accumulation and retention, but also label the development status of each Hypothesis as proved, 280 disproved or overlooked, a desirable design pattern for projects and Knowledge Management.

## 281

## **6 Discussions**

## 282 **6.1 Significance**

283 _Evident_ may advance many society domains such as software, philosophy, science, business as well 284 as Knowledge sharing and retention across the globe, thanks to its applicability to general KM.

285 _EKB_ may make no smaller impacts than relational data base [50], the invention of which created a 286 data base industry, as one solution to store information as Knowledge, a granular level above data.

## 287 **6.2 Work to do**

288 Work needs to be done regarding _Evident_ ergodicity over logical reasoning in philosophy. If proved, 289 _Evident_ can support all logical reasoning in philosophy. No evidence has existed to prove or disprove 290 the claim. _Evident_ ergodicity is overlooked, stated in _Evident_ language, especially considering logical 291 reasoning in philosophy may evolve.

292 Control studies need to be done to show _Evident_ can truly provide value. No tools tailored to support 293 _Evident_ project development planning and _EKB_ are available, although some existing tools are 294 applicable for use. Particularly the tools that allow unexpected alteration proof, easy access and 295 visualization of Containers are in demand. More detailed discussions need to be done about how 296 _Evident_ help applicable projects with examples.

## 297 **6.3 Pain points not alleviated**

298 _Evident_ offers no detailed guidance in development below Container level. For example, a Model or 299 computation flow cannot be broken down further into smaller modules by _Evident_ for incremental and 300 adaptive development. Multiple languages smells and accidents running prototype Algo in production 301 cannot be avoided by _Evident_ either. In addition, _Evident_ cannot control future observation alteration 302 caused by decisions made by people based on _Evident_ produced Knowledge.

303 _Evident_ can only manage project artifacts of data or codes but not sensors or hardwares. Pain points 304 caused in data acquisition such as availability, inaccuracy and inconsistency are out of _Evident_ ’s 305 scope. _Evident_ is incapable of improving computation hardware resources allocation either.

## 306 **6.4 More words about Agile**

307 Due to Agile’s ambiguity and unfalsifiability as a scientific claim, it might be a better practice to drop 308 the term Agile and instead quote each framework currently under Agile on its own. Frameworks 309 such as iterative and evolutionary development as well as Kanban are valuable although need to be 310 employed discretionally. Practitioners should have better understood what exactly they were doing 311 without being fuzzed by the buzzword Agile.

## 312

## **7 Conclusions**

313 The paper proposes _Evident_ as a project development and artifact management methodology for DM, 314 ML and KM as well as _EKB_ as a Knowledge base topology. _Evident_ and _EKB_ have been shown 315 of great value to alleviate many unresolved pain points. _Evident_ has the potential to facilitate the 316 advancement of many aspects of society due to its utility in general Knowledge management. _EKB_ 317 may serve as the infrastructure for storing information as Knowledge, a granular level above data.

9

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper proposes an ambitious interdisciplinary framework, *Evident* and *EKB*, aimed at addressing long-standing methodological gaps in data mining, machine learning, and knowledge management. The work is well-motivated, situating itself within a historical critique of Agile and Waterfall methodologies, and its attempt to bridge software engineering with epistemology and logical reasoning is compelling. However, the current manuscript faces significant clarity and positioning challenges: while the historical overview is thorough, the jump from broad historical observation to the proposed technical solution is currently too abstract. To improve, the author must explicitly define the "pain points" mentioned in the abstract that currently lack concrete specification, and provide a clearer distinction between *Evident* as a methodology and *EKB* as a physical/logical topology. Furthermore, the critique of Agile—specifically the accusation of "scientific unfalsifiability"—requires a more rigorous logical foundation to ensure the paper reads as a scholarly contribution rather than a philosophical polemic, as the current text risks alienating practitioners without yet providing the necessary empirical or formal evidence to replace existing industry standards.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated philosophical critique of Agile
comments.quote: Because falsifiability is the standard evaluating scientific against non-scientific claims introduced by Karl Popper [36], Agile is not a scientific claim.
comments.explanation: The authors define Agile as 'unfalsifiable'—and therefore scientifically invalid—based on a strict Popperian interpretation. This framing creates an overstatement, as the authors conflate the flexibility of a management framework with procedural failure. By centering the critique on 'falsifiability' in a domain (software management) where empirical case studies are the standard, the authors construct a 'straw man' argument that dismisses the methodology without addressing its practical application.

comments.title: Logical fallacy regarding Agile's applicability to DM
comments.quote: In a typical Agile development cycle (Fig 1b), Review phase is to discover Knowledge about user needs, which can be done through DM. Therefore Agile should not be applicable to DM, one phase of its own design cycle.
comments.explanation: The syllogism presented is a logical non-sequitur. The authors argue that because one phase of Agile (Review) utilizes a specific tool (DM), Agile as a whole cannot manage that tool. The suitability of a management framework for a task is determined by its ability to oversee the process, not by whether the task is functionally represented within that framework's lifecycle steps.

comments.title: Logical circularity in Deduction Knowledge definition
comments.quote: a Hypothesis associated Test that is also associated with an induction or abduction Knowledge Test represents deduction Knowledge or prediction (see Fig 3a&b).
comments.explanation: The definition is recursive: it relies on 'induction/abduction' objects that themselves contain a 'Test,' making the functional distinction between these categories ambiguous. This lack of a clear boundary between the definitions prevents a proper understanding of the methodology's implementation of 'prediction'.

comments.title: Ambiguity and versioning issues in 'Container' definition
comments.quote: Containers indicate Observations, Hypotheses and Tests can only be added or removed as a block.
comments.explanation: The atomic 'block' requirement for Containers creates significant ambiguity regarding state management. It is unclear if modifying a single component requires discarding the entire block's history, which threatens the reproducibility goals of the project. Furthermore, the interplay between atomic blocks and 'directional associations' is poorly defined, leading to confusion over whether individual elements can evolve independently.

comments.title: Logical constraints and scalability of EKB composition
comments.quote: For EKB s storing only induction and abduction Knowledge, all relational database operations are applicable...
comments.explanation: The methodology imposes a logical restriction: operations like 'Composition' are restricted to specific knowledge types. This creates a trap where users storing 'deduction' knowledge lose the ability to merge or scale their datasets effectively. The path for 'promoting' local induction knowledge into a central EKB containing complex deduction knowledge is not provided, hindering the methodology's stated collaboration goals.

comments.title: Logical gap in 'Evident ergodicity' claim
comments.quote: Work needs to be done regarding _Evident_ ergodicity over logical reasoning in philosophy.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'Evident ergodicity' as a core claim without defining it. Given the specific mathematical and physical definitions associated with 'ergodicity', its application to 'logical reasoning in philosophy' is opaque. Without a formal definition, the claim is unfalsifiable, mirroring the critique the authors levy against Agile.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance and automation claims
comments.quote: each project period develops a batch of independent Knowledges... to maximize unit time throughput
comments.explanation: The paper makes broad assertions regarding 'maximizing unit time throughput' and 'automatic documentation generation' without providing evidence, proof, or technical implementation details. These claims currently function as placeholders rather than substantiated research findings.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Overlooked' status mechanics
comments.quote: A Test can only be associated with one Observation, even if the associated Observation overlooks the Hypothesis(es)
comments.explanation: Binding a Test to a single 'overlooked' observation creates a potential logical trap where the system cannot incorporate subsequent data to resolve the status, effectively resulting in a permanent deadlock for that specific container.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding falsifiability and Agile
comments.quote: Because falsifiability is the standard evaluating scientific against non-scientific claims introduced by Karl Popper [36], Agile is not a scientific claim. Consequently, no observable scientific evidence can prove or disprove Agile, because technically no one can determine if a project is Agile or not in the first place.
comments.explanation: The author argues that Agile is 'unfalsifiable' because its definition is umbrella-like and lacks specific, actionable operational definitions. While philosophical critique of Agile's lack of formal specification is a valid point of discussion, the transition from 'vaguely defined' to 'unfalsifiable' is a leap. Many management frameworks and organizational methodologies (not just Agile) lack the rigorous falsifiability of natural science theories; framing the entire methodology as 'not a scientific claim' based on its current descriptive ambiguity—while true in a strict Popperian sense—tends to conflate a lack of process standardization with a failure of scientific validity. This is an overstatement that characterizes the methodology as scientifically invalid rather than merely loosely defined or difficult to measure.

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding Agile and Knowledge Discovery (DM)
comments.quote: Agile is also not applicable for Knowledge discovery tasks such as DM. In a typical Agile development cycle (Fig 1b), Review phase is to discover Knowledge about user needs, which can be done through DM. Therefore Agile should not be applicable to DM, one phase of its own design cycle.
comments.explanation: The logical conclusion that 'Agile should not be applicable to DM' because DM is used within an Agile 'Review' phase is flawed. This is a fallacy of composition or division. The fact that an iterative review process (Agile) might utilize a data discovery tool (DM) does not inherently preclude applying the broader iterative framework to the management of DM project tasks themselves. While the authors' intent is to show that DM, as an iterative and unpredictable research process, requires a different management approach, the syllogism provided—that a tool used within a design cycle cannot be the subject of that cycle—is logically weak.

comments.title: OCR-like typo in figure reference
comments.quote: the essence of Agile (see Fib 1b),
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Fib 1b' instead of 'Fig 1b'. Given that 'Fib' does not appear in the acronym list or context, this is a clear typo (likely an OCR artifact or mistype). While the context strongly implies 'Figure 1b', it is a persistent distraction for a reader checking the mentioned references.

comments.title: Logical non-sequitur in the critique of Agile's scope
comments.quote: In a typical Agile development cycle (Fig 1b), Review phase is to discover Knowledge about user needs, which can be done through DM. Therefore Agile should not be applicable to DM, one phase of its own design cycle.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that because one component of a methodology (the 'Review' phase in Agile) involves a process (Data Mining) that the methodology is arguably ill-suited for, the methodology *as a whole* should not be applicable to that process (DM). This is a logical fallacy: the suitability of a methodology for a task is determined by its ability to manage the requirements and delivery of that task, not by whether the task happens to overlap functionally with one phase of its lifecycle. A methodology can successfully oversee DM even if it contains a 'Review' phase that relies on DM outcomes. This argument undermines the authors' attempt to logically disqualify Agile from being useful in the DM domain.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding the falsifiability of Agile practitioners
comments.quote: If an Agile rollout “fails”, Agile proponents can always argue that the Agile rollout was not implemented correctly.
comments.explanation: The authors frame Agile's lack of precise, actionable steps as a 'falsifiability' problem, drawing on Popper. While it is true that Agile is a set of principles rather than a rigid protocol, the authors equate 'practice ambiguity' with 'unfalsifiability' in a way that is conceptually loose. The failure of a specific Agile project is usually measurable in terms of project constraints (time, budget, quality), even if the adherence to 'Agile' as a philosophy is debated. Classifying a methodology as 'unscientific' based on the variance in its practitioners' implementation is a significant polemic claim that requires more rigorous philosophical justification than provided here.

comments.title: Logical circularity in the definition of Deduction Knowledge
comments.quote: a Hypothesis associated Test that is also associated with an induction or abduction Knowledge Test represents deduction Knowledge or prediction (see Fig 3a&b).
comments.explanation: The text states that a Test associated with induction/abduction represents Deduction. However, earlier in the same paragraph, it defines induction/abduction as being composed of a Test, a Hypothesis, and an Observation. By nesting an induction/abduction object (which already contains a Test) inside the definition of a Deduction Test, the definition becomes recursive or circular without clearly explaining the functional distinction between a 'Deduction Test' and the 'Induction/Abduction Test' it consumes. This makes the methodology's implementation of 'prediction' ambiguous.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding project throughput
comments.quote: each project period develops a batch of independent Knowledges or Containers assigned to teams of various sizes to maximize unit time throughput
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the proposed Evident methodology will 'maximize unit time throughput' compared to existing methods. While this is a foundational claim for the utility of the methodology, no evidence or theoretical proof is provided to suggest why or how this modularity leads to higher throughput, especially given that the dependencies between Observations, Hypotheses, and Tests are hierarchical (as noted in section 4.2).

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Container' updates
comments.quote: Containers indicate Observations, Hypotheses and Tests can only be added or removed as a block.
comments.explanation: It is stated that a Container is a block of these three elements. If a user needs to update only the Observation to refine a Hypothesis, the text implies they must remove the entire 'block' and add it back. This creates an ambiguity regarding the persistence of historical records or versioning in the EKB: if the whole block is removed, is the previous state lost? Given that reproducibility is a key pain point the paper seeks to solve, this atomic handling needs more specific definition.

comments.title: Logical Tension in EKB Operational Consistency
comments.quote: For EKB s storing only induction and abduction Knowledge, all relational database operations are applicable, so that team members can compose their own EKB s without keeping a potentially large team EKB on the local machines.
comments.explanation: The authors define EKB operations such as 'Join' and 'Composition' as methods to merge knowledge bases. However, they previously state that 'operations dependent on values associations such as... Compositions... can only be implemented for EKB s storing only induction or abduction Knowledge'. This creates a logical dependency: if a team member creates a local EKB containing a deduction (prediction) knowledge, they effectively lose the ability to 'compose' it with the main EKB using these operations. The text implies this is a feature to support collaboration, but the restriction on the type of knowledge stored limits the scalability of such 'composition' without clearly explaining how one would safely 'promote' a local induction knowledge to a central EKB that contains complex deduction knowledge.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Overlooked' Deduction Logic
comments.quote: A Test can only be associated with one Observation, even if the associated Observation overlooks the Hypothesis(es) associated with the Test.
comments.explanation: The authors define 'overlooked' as a status where an Observation fails to either prove or disprove a hypothesis. However, the requirement that 'A Test can only be associated with one Observation' is problematic in a scientific context where the strength of a hypothesis is often derived from the aggregation of multiple observations. If a Test is permanently bound to a single, 'overlooked' observation, there is no mechanical path described to incorporate a second, subsequent observation to resolve the 'overlooked' status, effectively dead-locking that specific Test container. This restricts the methodology significantly more than the prose implies.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claim on Automated Documentation
comments.quote: Meanwhile once Containers are produced, Knowledge or documentation reports can be generated automatically instead of manually.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that Evident facilitates automatic report generation. While the EKB structure provides a formal schema (Observations/Hypotheses/Tests), the authors provide no detail on how the 'Knowledge' (the semantic significance of these associations) can be auto-generated into human-readable text without substantial additional metadata or natural language generation logic. This is an over-claim regarding the methodology's current state of readiness for 'automatic' documentation.

comments.title: Logical gap in the definition of 'Evident ergodicity'
comments.quote: Work needs to be done regarding _Evident_ ergodicity over logical reasoning in philosophy. If proved, _Evident_ can support all logical reasoning in philosophy. No evidence has existed to prove or disprove the claim.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the concept of 'Evident ergodicity' in Section 6.2 without defining it in the Notation or Definition sections. Given that 'ergodicity' has a specific mathematical definition (typically relating to time-averages equaling ensemble averages in stochastic processes) or a statistical mechanics definition, it is unclear what this term means in the context of 'logical reasoning in philosophy.' Without a formal definition, the claim that it 'can support all logical reasoning' is scientifically unfalsifiable—a critique the authors themselves levy against Agile.

comments.title: Use of 'unfalsifiability' vs. standard scientific practice
comments.quote: If an Agile rollout “fails”, Agile proponents can always argue that the Agile rollout was not implemented correctly.
comments.explanation: The authors define Agile as 'unfalsifiable' and therefore 'not a scientific claim' based on Popper's criteria. While this is a common critique of frameworks, the authors characterize this as a fatal flaw. However, in software engineering, methodology evaluation often relies on empirical evidence (case studies) rather than binary falsification. By framing this solely as a problem of falsifiability, the authors may be creating a 'straw man' argument, as most engineering methodology claims reside in applied science or management, not necessarily formal scientific theory.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Container' definition constraints
comments.quote: Containers indicate Observations, Hypotheses and Tests can only be added or removed as a block.
comments.explanation: The authors define Containers as atomic units ('added or removed as a block'). However, they later describe 'directional association constructions towards and only towards Test Containers.' It is unclear if an association between an Observation/Hypothesis and a Test constitutes an atomic 'Container' or if these associations are independent entities that violate the atomicity of the block when manipulated. This creates ambiguity regarding the granularity of version control when individual associations might need to evolve independently of the core Container.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a conceptual proposal for a new development methodology and knowledge base topology, but it suffers from several critical issues that undermine its suitability for a venue like NeurIPS. The most significant problem is the lack of concrete, testable contributions: the “Evident” methodology and “EKB” topology are described only in vague, high-level terms, with no formal definitions, algorithmic components, or empirical validation. The historical narrative, while extensive, does not connect to a clear, falsifiable hypothesis or a novel technical result—the paper reads more like a polemic against existing methodologies (Agile, CRISP-DM) than a constructive contribution to data mining, machine learning, or knowledge management. Clarity is further hampered by imprecise language and undefined jargon (e.g., “Knowledge” is defined as “knowledge supported by scientific evidence,” yet this is never operationalized), and the introduction conflates software development methodologies with data mining processes without justifying the transfer. For a conference like NeurIPS, which prizes rigorous theoretical or empirical advances, this paper fails to meet basic standards of scientific specificity and evidence; it would need a complete reframing toward a falsifiable framework, a concrete implementation, and a comparative evaluation to be considered viable.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 1 and narrative text regarding Agile popularity in the 1980s
comments.quote: |1980s|GUI & Internet|PC & Internet Applications|Waterfall standardized in military (1985)[20]; The Manifesto signed (2001)[24]. Agile signifcantly morepopular than Waterfall.|
comments.explanation: The table claims that in the 1980s Agile was significantly more popular than Waterfall. However, the surrounding text states that Waterfall was still widely deployed in the 1980s (lines 40-41) and that lightweight SDMs only emerged in the 1990s, with the Agile Manifesto signed in 2001 (lines 44-46). The text also says 'no clear preference between Waterfall and Agile variants was found in the literature' for the early 1970s (line 34) and implies Agile became de facto after 2001. This is a direct contradiction between the table's summary and the paper's own historical account. The table row also conflates the 1980s with events from 2001, making the claim about Agile popularity in the 1980s unsupported.

comments.title: Irrelevant mention of Von Neumann and Harvard architectures
comments.quote: While data and software’s storage manners differ between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures, data storage capacity growth empowered software to discover knowledge supported by scientific evidence
comments.explanation: The sentence introduces a contrast between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures, but this distinction is not relevant to the point about data storage capacity growth enabling knowledge discovery. The reader might wonder why architecture differences matter here — they are not defined, not used in the argument, and the author does not explain how they relate to storage capacity. This seems like a non-sequitur that could confuse readers expecting a clear link. The claim itself about differing storage manners is trivial and does not advance the discussion of data mining or methodology. It appears to be an unnecessary insertion that undermines the flow.

comments.title: Invalid inference: Agile being unfalsifiable does not entail it is not a scientific claim
comments.quote: Because falsifiability is the standard evaluating scientific against non-scientific claims introduced by Karl Popper [36], Agile is not a scientific claim.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because Agile is unfalsifiable, it is not a scientific claim. This conflates two distinct notions: (1) a claim being unfalsifiable vs. (2) a methodology or framework being a scientific claim. Even if we grant that the statement 'this project follows Agile' is unfalsifiable (due to vague definitions), that does not make Agile itself a scientific claim. A scientific claim would be something like 'Agile improves productivity by 20%'. The authors seem to be arguing that because the definition of Agile is vague, no empirical test can refute a claim of being Agile. That is logically valid. However, the conclusion 'Agile is not a scientific claim' is imprecise: it is the assertion of being an Agile practice that is unfalsifiable, not the methodology's content. The prose overstates what has been shown.

comments.title: Unsupported leap from 'vague approaches' to 'impossible to determine if a practice is Agile'
comments.quote: Therefore although there are numerous frameworks under the Agile umbrella [34, 35], it's impossible to determine if a development practice is Agile and the claim of Agile practice becomes unfalsifiable.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because the Manifesto's values and principles are vague, it is impossible to determine if any practice is Agile. However, many frameworks (Scrum, XP, etc.) have concrete practices and definitions. The fact that the umbrella term has fuzzy boundaries does not entail that no practice can be classified as Agile; there exist clear examples (e.g., daily standups, sprints) that are widely accepted as Agile. The argument that 'impossible to determine' is too strong. A more careful claim would be 'difficult to determine with certainty' or 'the boundaries are fuzzy'. This is an overstatement.

comments.title: Non sequitur: Agile not applicable to DM because DM is a phase of its own cycle
comments.quote: Agile is also not applicable for Knowledge discovery tasks such as DM. In a typical Agile development cycle (Fig 1b), Review phase is to discover Knowledge about user needs, which can be done through DM. Therefore Agile should not be applicable to DM, one phase of its own design cycle.
comments.explanation: This reasoning is flawed. The fact that Agile uses DM as part of its Review phase does not logically preclude using Agile to manage a DM project. For example, software development uses compilation as a phase, but that doesn't mean we can't use Agile to manage a compiler development project. The argument confuses the use of a technique (DM) within a methodology with the methodology applied to that technique. The conclusion does not follow from the premise. This is a logical error.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about Agile and unfalsifiability
comments.quote: Consequently, no observable scientific evidence can prove or disprove Agile, because technically no one can determine if a project is Agile or not in the first place.
comments.explanation: Even if the definition of Agile is vague, that does not preclude empirical studies comparing specific Agile practices (e.g., Scrum vs. Waterfall) using operationalized definitions. Many empirical software engineering studies have compared Agile methods to traditional methods with measurable outcomes. The authors' claim that 'no observable scientific evidence can prove or disprove Agile' is an overstatement; it would be more accurate to say that studies on 'Agile as a whole' face definitional challenges. This claim is too sweeping without considering the large body of empirical research on Agile (e.g., Dybå & Dingsøyr 2008).

comments.title: Unclear referent for 'Appendix'
comments.quote: Most of the Values and Principles appear to be goals but not approaches (see Appendix); some are concerned with approaches but vaguely defined.
comments.explanation: The text references an 'Appendix' that is not provided in the excerpt. While this is a forward reference that might exist in the full paper, the reader cannot verify the claim that 'only four principles are actionable'. This is a minor oversight in the context of the passage being checked, as we cannot confirm the accuracy of the count without the appendix. However, since this is a typical cross-reference in academic papers, it is not a serious error unless the appendix is missing.

comments.title: Table 2 appears duplicated in passage
comments.quote: Table 2: Pain Points of DM,ML and KM.
comments.explanation: Table 2 appears to be inserted twice: once after line 107 and again after line 108 (which begins the section '3 Pain points for DM, ML and KM'). The duplication suggests a copy-paste error during OCR or manuscript preparation. While not a logical error, it is confusing for the reader. However, this may be an artifact of the PDF extraction rather than the authors' intention. Given the instruction to be lenient with formatting, this is minor.

comments.title: Misuse of 'predicated' instead of 'predicted'
comments.quote: Future Knowledge predicated by the deployed Algo may encourage Algo users to alter decisions
comments.explanation: The word 'predicated' means 'based on' (e.g., 'predicated on the assumption'), not 'forecast' or 'predicted'. The context clearly discusses knowledge produced by an algorithm deployed for prediction, so the intended word is 'predicted'. This is likely an OCR misread of 'predicted'. The error changes the meaning from a future forecast to a logical foundation, which could mislead readers about the feedback-loop argument.

comments.title: Undefined and potentially misused term 'ergodicity'
comments.quote: Work needs to be done regarding _Evident_ ergodicity over logical reasoning in philosophy. If proved, _Evident_ can support all logical reasoning in philosophy.
comments.explanation: The term 'ergodicity' is introduced without any definition or prior context in the paper. In mathematics and statistical mechanics, ergodicity refers to the property that time averages equal ensemble averages. Here it seems to be used in a different, unspecified sense—possibly to mean that Evident can cover or reproduce all forms of logical reasoning. The authors do not explain what 'Evident ergodicity over logical reasoning' means or how it would be proved. This ambiguity could seriously mislead readers who are familiar with the standard meaning of the term, and it leaves a central claim (that Evident could support all logical reasoning) unsupported. The context does not resolve the meaning because the term appears only in this passage and is not discussed elsewhere in the provided sections.

comments.title: Overstated conclusion of demonstrated value
comments.quote: _Evident_ and _EKB_ have been shown of great value to alleviate many unresolved pain points.
comments.explanation: The paper provides a conceptual argument that Evident and EKB can alleviate certain pain points (Table 2), but it does not present any empirical validation, controlled studies, or quantitative results. The phrase 'have been shown of great value' implies a stronger level of evidence than actually exists in the paper, which itself calls for control studies in Section 6.2. While this may be acceptable as a forward-looking conclusion in a proposal paper, it risks misleading readers into believing that the methodology has been empirically validated. The earlier text (Section 4) describes the alleviation as 'demonstrated conceptually,' which is more accurate.
